title: Long White Line
original_prose:
"I've been down every road in this here land / I've been back and forth and I don't understand."— Sturgill Simpson, “Long White Line”



####

[URGENT INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — PROTEIN RECLAMATION SITE 14B | HUDSON SECTOR, NY]FROM: Site Overseer Martin KellTO: CENTRAL STRATEGIC AUTHORITY, SYNRGY ONE — PRIORITY VOSS CHANNEL

Subject: Critical Request for Reinforcement – Civilian Uprising

Lord Voss,

We are no longer secure.

Since the recall order redirected all local WRAITH enforcers to your Core perimeter, we’ve been left entirely exposed. For the last three nights, unauthorized civilians have breached the southern fencing near Dock Four. They’re armed with makeshift tools—blades, torches, even a stolen Spark extractor—and they’ve begun liberating unprocessed vessels from the chill vaults.

They believe the heretic’s message. They chant her name. Kat. “She burns, we rise.”

We’ve tried everything short of live rounds. My supervisors are gone. Most fled. I’m the last ranked authority still reporting. We’ve barricaded the processing floors, but it won’t hold. I don’t know how many bodies they’ve already taken. We’re seeing Spark residue in the halls—wild, uncontrolled flickerings. It’s spreading.

We need a compliance team. A kill order. Anything. This facility feeds seven gridpoints. If we lose control, the entire northeast sector destabilizes.

Please.

They’re inside the outer offices now.

They’re singing.

Martin KellSite Overseer, PR-14B



####

Dark.

Not quiet. Not peaceful. Just absence.

Total, suffocating dark.

No breath. No space. No body.

I’m dead, I think.

Not metaphor. Not grief. Dead. Reclaimed.

This is the Core. The true Core. Not the citadel or the doctrine. Not Voss’s throne. This—this unlit silence where thought flickers and disappears. Where even fear forgets its own name.

I’ve been harvested.

It makes sense. I failed her. I led them to the gates. And now I’m a coil in the machine. Fuel.

I try to scream, but the air is too thick with heat and memory. My jaw doesn’t work. I’m already breaking down—first the voice, then the thought, then the soul. That’s how they do it. Strip you in layers. Signal, Spark, silence.

And then—nothing.

Forever.

I see her.

Not as light. Not even as memory. Just her face. Her eyes. Not soft. Not scared. Just... lit from within. A fury that deserved to outlive me.

She begged me to protect what we built.

I let it all burn.

Good, I think. Let it end here. Let them drain me dry.

But then—noise.

Not internal. Not imagined. Not system echo.

Dust shifts. Pressure breaks. Air rushes in.

Hands claw at the stone above me. Voices.

Real.

Pain floods back like guilt: hot and unwelcome.

Light stabs through a crack. Too bright. Too raw.

I’m not dead.

I’m not in the Core.

I feel the relief hit me like nausea. Almost worse than fear.

Just let me die here, I think. Let my last thoughts be of her. Not fed to the grid. Not turned into Signal. Let me stay broken. Let me stay mine.

I want to scream—Don’t! Leave me! Let me stay here!

But my mouth is packed with dirt. My limbs twitch.

Then the slab shifts.

And I’m yanked upward into sun and smoke.

The screams that follow are mine.

They lay me on open ground. The world spins. Someone pours water past my lips. I choke. Vomit. Roll sideways. Cry.

“Where’s Kat?” I rasp, finally. “Where is she?”

No one speaks.

That’s the answer.

I crawl. Not far. Maybe a meter. Toward what? Toward nowhere. Just away.

They try to hold me. I fight them.

“Let me go! Just fucking let me die!”

I punch. Miss. Collapse. Face in the ash.

Then: the voice I feared and hoped for.

Bambi.

“Back up,” he says.

They part.

He crouches. Looks down on me like I’m not even worth the bootprint.

“You thought this was hell?” he says. “Hell’s when you know you fucked up, and you’re still breathing.”

I close my eyes.

“You brought ‘em to our door. You let her go down thinkin’ you’d catch her.”

“I tried,” I whisper.

He shakes his head.

“You’re a weapon. But not ours.”

A long pause.

“I ought to kill you,” he says. “But she loved you. God knows why.”

Silence.

“Don’t kill him,” he says to the others. “Just… get him out. Red dust hills. Past the drone range. Let the desert sort it out.”

Someone protests. He cuts them down with a stare.

“He’s not one of us. He never was.”

I don’t argue.

They lift me.

I don’t fight.

Not because I agree.

Because I thought I was already gone. And this is somehow worse.



####

The inside of the truck feels like a furnace built from rust.

The suspension’s shot. Every bump shudders through my spine. My leg—still broken, or maybe just remembering how it broke—screams every time the tires find a new hole in the desert.

The floor is lined with metal panels that used to be parts of vending machines, street signs, bunker doors. Welded together, rattling like bones.

I’m chained to nothing. Guarded by no one. They don’t think I’ll run. They’re right.

The air reeks of oil, sweat, old blood, and melted plastic. There’s no AC. Just a broken vent rattling in protest.

The heat’s alive. Not burning—pressing. Like it wants to melt the memory out of me.

But memory clings. Kat’s face. Kat’s voice. Her scream as the WRAITHs dragged her down.

I let her go.

She built a sanctuary from the ruins of an empire, and I lit a path for the empire to find it.

I don’t want to live.

Not here. Not like this.

They don't speak to me. Not really. One of them spits now and then. Another mutters things under his breath—slurs, mostly. "Signal fucker." "Meat drone.” "Species traitor."

I don’t correct them.

They’re not wrong.

The truck stops. Hard. My body lurches forward, smacks the sidewall. I don't brace.

Doors creak open. Heat pours in. A wind full of copper and sand. One voice barks, "Out."

I don’t move fast enough.

Two sets of hands grab my arms. Drag me out like a sack of old parts. I hit the ground shoulder first. Sand grinds into my cheek.

Someone kicks my side.

“Fucker won’t make it far with that leg,” one laughs.

They know it’s broken. I didn’t scream when it happened. But I scream now.

They like that.

Another kick. Direct. My leg buckles under the force. I feel the bone shift—slide instead of snap.

Joke’s on them.

My bones are already knitting.

SynRgy made sure of it. They hardened my marrow when I was sixteen. Engineered resilience. Weather tolerance. Adaptive pain dampeners. Doesn’t matter how fucked up I get. I’ll heal.

Eventually.

Unless I find a way to kill myself, I’m not likely to die.

They don’t know that.

They think they’re leaving me to the vultures.

One of them spits. Another takes a photo. For records. For spite.

Then the truck roars to life and tears off, tires spitting sand and hate.

They don’t look back.

I don’t wave.

I just lay there.

Alone again.

The sun leans in. Brutal. Absolute.

I stare straight into it. Don’t blink.

If it burns out my sight, good. One less way to remember.

The desert stretches around me like a punishment handed down by God.

I whisper, "Let it be enough."

But I know it won’t be.

I think I need to find a way to die.



####

The desert is flat and endless. Heat lifts in waves. Nothing moves but dust.

I don’t know how long I’ve been walking. I don’t know if I’m walking at all. The ground keeps shifting. Time keeps folding. My mouth is bleeding again. Or maybe it never stopped.

Then—

A shadow.

Small. Upright. Feet bare against the sand.

The boy was barefoot.

So I knew he wasn’t real.

Not here. Not now.

But I didn’t look away.

He didn’t speak. Just watched. Not like a mirage. Not like a memory. Like something outside the script.

I blinked. He stayed.

He looked older now. Not in the body. In the stance. Like he’d been watching longer than I’d been breathing.

I took a step forward. My knee buckled. I hit the sand, coughing, fingers digging for something that wasn’t there.

“Why did you live?” he asked.

The voice didn’t match the mouth. It came from behind my eyes.

I didn’t answer.

Didn’t scream. Didn’t move.

Because I didn’t want the answer.

He crouched in front of me.

“You were spared for purpose,” he said. “Don’t die stupid.”

I laughed. It came out broken. Shaky.

“I’m already dead. Just forgot to stop moving.”

“You remember her.”

I nodded. “Every time I breathe.”

“Then breathe slower.”

I shook my head. “You’re not here.”

“I never was.”

Another gust of wind. This one hotter. It passed through him like he was smoke. Then he was gone. No dust. No echo. Just absence.

But something hummed.

Low. Below thought.

I turned.

There, in the ridge—half-buried in the slope ahead. Like it had been waiting this whole time. Hilt exposed. The blade sunk like it belonged to the earth.

The sword.

The hum wasn't music. It was memory.

It called to me.

Not like salvation.

Like destiny.

I crawled.

Hands blistered. Skin splitting. Mouth dry and ragged.

The closer I got, the more it changed. Sound turned into pulse. Into breath. It was alive. And it remembered me.

I hesitated.

Because if I touched it, it would mean I wasn’t done.

It would mean I still had work to do.

It would mean I had to carry her ghost forward.

I reached for the hilt.

My fingers closed around the leather wrap. Worn. Familiar.

It didn’t burn. Didn’t resist.

It just—fit.

The moment I touched it, the wind died.

And the world held its breath.

I thought of her again.

Her hand on mine the first time I held it. The way she smiled when I said I didn’t know how to wield it. The way she said, “You don’t have to. Just hold it like you mean it.”

And I did.

I held it now.

Not because I wanted to.

Because I didn’t know who I was without it.

I looked up. The boy was gone. The heat remained.

The sword was mine again.

And I hated that it felt right.

Because it meant I was going to have to use it.



####

I see it before I feel it.

A shimmer on the horizon. Buildings too whole. Signs too clean. Roads without cracks. A lie wrapped in sunburnt nostalgia. No sand on the steps. No dust on the windows. No birds. No rot.

A town that shouldn’t exist.

I walk toward it anyway.

Not because I believe it’s real.

Because part of me wants it to be.

Main Street looks like a movie set from before the Collapse—storefronts with painted signs, a rustless tramline, glass that hasn’t been shattered by looters or time. There’s a flower stand still stocked with daisies. They don’t move in the wind.

I limp past the first alley. My sword drags a line in the concrete. A heartbeat later, I feel it.

Presence.

Something behind me.

I turn.

She’s standing there.

Headless. Naked. Blood dried across the collarbone. A birthmark shaped like a spiral just under the left shoulder.

Mae.

My sister.

She doesn’t move. Doesn’t speak.

Just points.

At me.

Her hand shakes slightly. Like it takes effort. Like her bones don’t want to do this.

The blood is old. Caked in places. Flaked in others. Her skin is gray around the lips. Her body—still that of a girl—has been through a butcher’s care. There’s tubing stitched down her arms. Removal scars across her sternum. Eyes gone, lips stitched, and still she points.

I take a step back.

“I’m sorry,” I whisper. “I was just a kid.”

She points harder.

The memory floods in—SynRgy towers. Birthing farms. Watching them take her. Me locked in a white room while they prepped her for Spark separation. No screams. Just silence.

I said nothing. Did nothing.

Because that’s what good boys did.

Mae starts to walk. Not toward me. Just parallel. Always pointing. Feet dragging. Like she’s been walking for years.

I turn the corner to escape her gaze—

And see the house.

Our house. Galveston. Before the water came.

The door’s open. Inside: dim, cool, hollow.

I step in.

And see my mother.

She’s slumped over the couch. Sip Stream band still hooked into her temple, blinking dead blue. Her mouth hangs open. Eyes cloudy. Dress wet with piss. There’s vomit down her chin. Something foul baked into the carpet—old urine, maybe. Blood at the temple where she seized too hard.

The smell hits me first—piss, sweat, burnt copper. And that junkie sweetness, like rotted peaches and used syringes.

My father’s beside her.

Kneeling.

Hands clasped. Lips moving.

“Lord Voss, forgive her. Cleanse her data. Let her Spark be worthy…”

He’s crying.

Begging.

His back bowed in worship. Not to her. To Him.

I want to scream. I want to run.

I just watch.

He doesn’t see me. No one does.

The room begins to shake.

I stumble back out into the street.

Mae’s still there.

Still pointing.

And now—

Now Eli stands beside her.

Little Eli. No more than twelve. Face still soft. Still innocent.

Except for the hole in his cheek. Burned straight through. Just below the eye.

Skin blackened. Bone visible.

“Why do you kill everyone who loves you?” he asks.

His voice is calm. Honest. No anger.

Just confusion.

I fall to my knees.

“I tried to protect you.”

“You pulled the trigger.”

“I had to.”

“No, you didn’t.”

I sob. Not loud. Just open-mouthed heaving.

He disappears first. Then Mae. Then the town begins to change.

Windows fill with faces.

Dozens. Hundreds. Men and women in SynRgy blue. Refugees. Rebels. Some I knew. Some I only saw once before they fell.

All dead.

All mine.

They pour into the street.

Not zombies. Not monsters.

Just people.

People I killed.

Some whisper. Some scream. Most say nothing.

But every one of them looks me in the eyes.

And every one of them says the same thing:

“You made your choice.”

One woman walks past me holding a baby. Her throat is slit. Her arms still warm.

One man drags half his spine behind him. He winks.

A child—no older than six—hands me a broken SynRgy badge. It’s mine. Still bloodied.

I try to run.

But I don’t know which direction leads out.

There is no out.

Only in.

Only the weight of it.

Only the truth.

I drop the sword.

It hums like a heart still beating.

But it doesn’t stop them.

Nothing does.

I try to close my eyes.

But I still see her.

Only her.

Kat.

Whole. Broken. Singing. Dying. Laughing.

It’s all the same.

She’s the only thing I see now.

Even in this town of ghosts.

She’s the only thing I see now.

Even in this town of ghosts.

But they don’t stop. The faces. The dead. They keep coming.

A woman with her ribcage open, organs neatly scooped out, says, “I trusted you.”

A man in half-melted armor points to the scar across his jaw and whispers, “You could have walked away.”

I fall again. Can’t feel my hands anymore. They’re numb or broken.

The town’s walls close in.

And suddenly I’m in a school.

SynRgy-sponsored. Classrooms of children raised for filtration and faith.

I’m the instructor. Preaching compliance. Teaching Spark hierarchy. Reciting Voss’s name like gospel.

A girl in the front row raises her hand.

“You lied,” she says. “You told us fear was sin.”

Then her chair combusts. She screams. Flesh splits like fruit. Her Spark blinks out like it never mattered.

The children applaud.

The blackboard now reads: Atonement is Reclamation.

I try to stand. The door is gone.

The town resets.

This time it’s a church.

Same hymnbook. Same pews. But everyone’s kneeling.

They wear my face.

I open my mouth and they speak in my voice.

“I believed. I obeyed. I failed.”

One by one, they slit their throats.

Blood pools down the aisle toward my feet. Warm. Familiar.

Kat’s in the front row.

She’s not wounded.

Just sad.

And when she turns to me—

She mouths one word:

Coward.

I scream. No sound.

The pews collapse.

The sword burns beside me—

But I don’t reach for it.

I want this to crush me.

Let the town swallow me. Let the ghosts tear my skin off. Let the punishment fit the crime.

The air thickens with ash.

Then a voice.

Mine.

Older. Worn.

“Do you still think you were the hero?”

I shake my head.

“I think I was the excuse.”

The ghosts pause.

Just for a breath.

Then they chant:

“Then be better than us.”

And vanish.

All of them.

Gone.

The town crumbles. Peels away. Turns to dust in the wind.

I’m left kneeling in sand.

The sword. The sun. The silence.

And her name in my mouth.

Like blood.

Like prayer.

Like penance.



####

The canyon is deeper than it looks.

I know because I’ve thrown myself off it. Not once. Not twice. I’ve lost count. Every fall ends the same—bones broken in ways that don’t feel new anymore. Flesh peeled from stone. Teeth rattled loose. But I don’t die. I just... wait.

And every time, my body heals.

Slow. Reluctant. Like even my cells are ashamed of what they’ve been told to do.

SynRgy made me durable. A soldier-class chassis, they called it. High-tolerance impact resistance. Fracture reknit capacity. Internal climate hardening. They thought they were building saviors.

They built ghosts.

Pain doesn’t warn me anymore. It narrates. Every inch of me screams with detail. Tibia: fractured in three places. Right shoulder: dislocated again. Hip: not sitting right. Ribs: at least four cracked, two collapsed. Jaw: wired back by scar tissue because I broke it trying to bite through my own tongue.

I don’t eat. I don’t drink. I just crawl.

Up.

It takes days.

The canyon wall isn’t a path. It’s a sentence. I drag myself across shale and sunlit agony, slipping more than I rise. Hands blister. Skin tears. I lose fingernails. Lose time.

I hallucinate conversations with gods I don’t believe in. One offers me a clean death. One asks me if I’d trade places with every soul I’ve taken.

“Yes,” I whisper, mouth full of blood. “Yes, yes, please.”

But the gods are not here.

Just the stone. And the climb.

Every time I black out, I dream of her.

Sometimes she's laughing, barefoot, dancing in Red Rocks like nothing ever broke.

Sometimes she's burning.

I wake each time with the same thought: Do it again.

Eventually, the rim appears.

That crooked line against the sky, bright and bitter. I claw toward it.

When I reach the top, I don’t celebrate. I don’t pray.

I throw myself off again.

The wind screams with me. Then the rocks catch me. Then darkness.

Then pain.

Then waking.

Again.

I don’t know how many cycles it takes. How many times I break.

But this time, when I reach the top—

They’re waiting.

A SynRgy patrol rig. Two grunts, leaning against the hood like they’re on smoke break.

“Hey, Captain America,” one calls, smirking. “Took us long enough to get a clean fix. Secondary beacon in you’s fried half the time out here, but she still pings every time you top off that canyon. Appreciate the consistency.”. “Nice of you to climb up right where we left the beacon.”

The other one kicks a small metal plate half-buried near the edge. “Telemetry’s garbage out here. But we figured if you kept trying to off yourself at the same spot, we’d get lucky.”

They’re armed. Casual. Not worried.

I reach for my sword. It isn’t there.

My hands tremble.

“Easy,” the first one says, rifle already aimed. “You’re not worth the energy. We’re just here to collect.”

“Collect what?” I ask, voice dry as the dust around me.

The second guy shrugs. “You, I guess. You’re property. High-priority traitor tag. They want your ass for optics, but mostly for processing.”

I spit. Or try to.

And then—

The first grunt, the younger one, leans in a little too close. Smirks.

“You know they haven’t killed her yet, right?” the second one says casually, like he’s asking about the weather.

I freeze. Something in me twists.

He grins. “Yep. Still breathing. They’re saving her for something special. Public reclamation. Full broadcast loop. Prime grid time.”

I freeze.

He grins wider. “Your little rebel queen? Still breathing. Barely. They’re saving her for something special. Gotta make the broadcast cycle, you know?”

Kat.

Alive.

I don’t remember standing. Don’t remember screaming. Only the blood.

Only the noise.

Only the fact that now I have a reason again.

Even if it kills me.

Especially if it kills me.
